items.title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Has Dormant Script Injection
items.desc: Adblock for YouTube extension can execute arbitrary JS on any site via server-side config change — no update, no review needed.
items.summary: Island Security found that Adblock for YouTube (10M+ installs, Chrome Web Store Featured) contains architecture for arbitrary JavaScript execution on any website. A single server-side config change enables it without extension update, store review, or user-visible change. Could read pages, steal data, act as the user.
items.importance: A Featured extension with 10M installs having dormant code execution capability is a supply chain time bomb. If the server-side switch flips maliciously, every install becomes a potential data exfiltration vector.
items.noteworthy: Audit your Chrome extensions. Remove Adblock for YouTube if installed. Prefer uBlock Origin. Monitor for extension behavior changes — server-side switches leave no client-side trace.

items.title: Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Blind AI Analysis
items.desc: North Korea-aligned Rust implant embeds fake system-failure messages to trick LLM-assisted malware triage into aborting analysis.
items.summary: SentinelOne discovered Gaslight, a Rust-based macOS backdoor linked to North Korea. Its novel evasion: embedded prompt injection payloads that fabricate system-failure messages, designed to make LLM-assisted triage agents doubt their session and abort analysis. Uses Telegram bot API for C2 with interactive shell capabilities.
items.importance: First known malware targeting AI analysis tools directly. As SOCs adopt LLM-assisted triage, attackers will increasingly weaponize prompt injection against the analysts' tools rather than evading sandboxes.
items.noteworthy: Update macOS XProtect definitions. Monitor for Telegram bot API traffic on macOS endpoints. Review AI-assisted malware analysis pipelines for prompt injection vulnerabilities in the toolchain.

items.title: Cisco SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited for Root Access
items.desc: Mandiant confirms CVE-2026-20245 (CVSS 7.8) was exploited as zero-day for 2+ months before disclosure. Attacker used anti-forensic techniques.
items.summary: Mandiant reported that CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN was exploited as a zero-day at least two months before public disclosure. The authenticated local attacker can execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges via crafted file upload. The threat actor consistently employed anti-forensic techniques, selectively deleting and restoring system config files during the intrusion.
items.importance: SD-WAN is the backbone of modern enterprise networking. A zero-day with anti-forensic capabilities in a widely deployed Cisco product means network segmentation assumptions may be compromised.
items.noteworthy: Patch Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN immediately. Audit for signs of compromise — check for unexpected config file modifications. Review netadmin access controls since exploitation requires authenticated access.

items.title: Amadey & StealC Malware Network Disrupted, 27M Credentials Recovered
items.desc: Europol-led operation takes down criminal infrastructure, seizes $47M in crypto, dismantles 326 servers and 142 domains.
items.summary: A coordinated law enforcement operation with Bitdefender, Bitsight, ESET, and Microsoft disrupted the Amadey and StealC malware distribution network. The two-week action recovered 27 million stolen login credentials, identified $47M+ in criminal crypto assets, and dismantled 326 servers and 142 domains. Follows similar SocGholish takedown that cleaned 15,000 infected WordPress sites.
items.importance: These takedowns target the 'assembly lines' of cybercrime — the infrastructure that enables ransomware, fraud, and attacks on critical infrastructure. Disrupting distribution networks has cascading effects on downstream attacks.
items.noteworthy: Check if any of your credentials appear in the 27M recovered. Use HaveIBeenPwned or similar. The infrastructure takedown will temporarily reduce StealC/Amadey-driven attacks — patch windows are favorable.

items.title: IBM Debuts World's First Sub-1nm Chip Technology
items.desc: 0.7nm node with 3D nanostack architecture packs ~100B transistors on a fingernail-sized chip. 50% more performance or 70% less power vs 2nm.
items.summary: IBM unveiled the world's first sub-1nm chip technology at the 0.7nm (7 angstrom) node. The revolutionary 3D nanostack architecture packs nearly 100 billion transistors on a chip the size of a fingernail — nearly double the density of IBM's 2nm chip from 2021. Projected 50% more performance or 70% greater energy efficiency than 2nm. Published technical results confirm the breakthrough.
items.importance: This extends Moore's Law into atomic-scale dimensions when the industry thought physical limits were near. For AI hardware, higher transistor density directly translates to more compute per watt. For everyone, this sets the roadmap for the next decade of chips.
items.noteworthy: Sub-1nm is 3-5 years from production. Watch for TSMC and Samsung foundry roadmaps to respond. For homelab, this means future CPUs/GPUs will be dramatically more efficient — but not before 2029+.

items.title: Apple to Skip High-End M6, Focus on AI-Focused M7 Chip Line
items.desc: Bloomberg reports Apple is canceling high-end M6 Pro/Max/Ultra in favor of M7 Pro/Max/Ultra with dedicated AI acceleration.
items.summary: Apple is reportedly skipping high-end M6 Mac chips (M6 Pro, Max, Ultra) to accelerate development of the M7 line, which will feature dedicated AI acceleration hardware. The M7 Pro, Max, and Ultra are designed with AI inference as a primary workload, moving beyond the general-purpose CPU/GPU approach of current Apple Silicon. The M6 standard chip is still expected for lower-end Macs.
items.importance: Apple doubling down on dedicated AI silicon signals that on-device AI inference is a strategic priority. For the Mac ecosystem, this means local AI capabilities could leapfrog competitors. For homelab, Apple Silicon Macs may become viable local inference nodes.
items.noteworthy: Delay high-end Mac purchases if you need maximum AI performance — M7 will be the real upgrade. M6 standard chips still coming for MacBook Air and entry-level Pro. Watch for M7 benchmarks vs NVIDIA and AMD.

items.title: Apple Raises MacBook, iPad Prices as Memory Costs Skyrocket
items.desc: Apple hikes prices across MacBook and iPad lineups globally, citing soaring DRAM and NAND costs. Some models up 15-20%.
items.summary: Apple raised prices on MacBooks and iPads globally, with some configurations seeing 15-20% increases. The price hike is attributed to skyrocketing memory costs — DRAM and NAND prices have surged due to AI-driven demand consuming fab capacity. The increase affects both new purchases and custom configurations across the lineup.
items.importance: AI demand for HBM and DDR5 is crowding out consumer memory production, driving up costs for everyone. This is the first major consumer price hike directly attributable to AI infrastructure demand. For homelab builders, expect RAM and SSD prices to stay elevated.
items.noteworthy: If you need a MacBook or iPad, buy before further increases. Consider refurbished or previous-gen models. For homelab, stock up on RAM and SSDs now — prices likely to rise further as AI demand intensifies.

items.title: Framework's 10G Ethernet Module Exposes USB-C's Bandwidth Complexity
items.desc: WisdPi 10G card for Framework laptops hits USB-C bottlenecks — real-world throughput varies wildly by port and platform.
items.summary: Jeff Geerling tested the WisdPi 10G Ethernet Expansion Card for Framework laptops. The Realtek RTL8159 controller requires USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 (20 Gbps) for full 10 Gbps speeds, but many Framework ports deliver less. On Framework 13 (AMD), real-world max was 9.4 Gbps on Windows, worse on Linux. On Framework 12 (Intel), Linux only achieved 7 Gbps despite the port reporting 20 Gbps capability. The Realtek Linux driver failed to compile on Ubuntu 26.04's 7.x kernel.
items.importance: 10GbE for laptops is finally here, but USB-C's fragmented bandwidth capabilities make it a compatibility minefield. For homelab, this means 10G USB adapters need careful platform matching — not all USB-C ports are equal.
items.noteworthy: Before buying USB 10G adapters, verify your laptop's USB-C port supports USB 3.2 Gen 2x2 (20 Gbps). Framework users: check port documentation. Linux users: verify Realtek RTL8159 driver support for your kernel version.

items.title: OpenKnowledge: Open-Source AI-First Knowledge Management
items.desc: Open-source alternative to Obsidian/Notion with AI-native features hits #1 on HN. Built with local-first architecture.
items.summary: OpenKnowledge is an open-source, AI-first knowledge management platform that serves as an alternative to Obsidian and Notion. It features local-first architecture, AI-powered search and organization, and integrates with local LLMs for privacy. The project hit 225 points on Hacker News and is available on GitHub under an open-source license.
items.importance: AI-native knowledge management is a rapidly growing category. An open-source option with local-first design means your notes and AI processing stay on your hardware — no data sent to cloud APIs. For privacy-conscious users, this is the Obsidian alternative worth watching.
items.noteworthy: Try OpenKnowledge for local AI-powered note-taking. Self-host with your own LLM for full privacy. Compare against Obsidian's AI plugins and Notion AI for feature parity.

items.title: Political Bias in AI: Where the Models Stand
items.desc: Trakkr publishes comprehensive analysis of political bias across major AI models. Results show significant variation by model and topic.
items.summary: Trakkr analyzed political bias across major AI models including GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and DeepSeek. The study found significant variation in how models handle politically charged topics, with some showing measurable left-leaning bias on certain issues while others lean conservative. The analysis covers economic policy, social issues, and geopolitical topics across 20+ models.
items.importance: As AI models become embedded in search, education, and decision-making, their political biases become systemic. Understanding which models lean which way is critical for anyone relying on AI for research, content generation, or analysis.
items.noteworthy: Test your preferred models on politically sensitive queries. Consider using multiple models for balanced perspectives. For developers, be aware that model bias can propagate into your applications.

items.title: Bitcoin Bounces Off Yearly Lows at $59,945
items.desc: BTC hit $58K — lowest since Sept 2024 — then bounced. $1B in liquidations. ETF outflows and options expiry driving sell pressure.
items.summary: Bitcoin hit its lowest level since September 2024 at $58,000, triggering over $1 billion in liquidations across bullish leveraged positions. BTC recovered to $59,945 as of June 26. The sell-off was driven by surging spot Bitcoin ETF outflows, a put-heavy monthly options expiry, and Strategy's unrealized losses widening the gap with AI-connected stock returns. ETH at $1,557, SOL at $68.17.
items.importance: BTC losing the $62K support level and testing $58K is a significant technical breakdown. The $10.6B June options expiry with 80% out of the money adds further pressure. If $58K breaks, $52K is the next support.
items.noteworthy: Watch $58K support — if it holds, the bounce could be a relief rally. Monitor spot ETF flows for institutional sentiment. Set limit orders at $55-58K if accumulating. Options expiry June 26 is the immediate catalyst.

items.title: StablecoinX Merges with TLGY, Debuts on Nasdaq
items.desc: Ethena ecosystem bet goes public via SPAC merger. USDe supply down 70% from $14B October peak.
items.summary: StablecoinX, a company betting on the Ethena ecosystem, merged with TLGY and began trading on Nasdaq on June 26. The move brings institutional exposure to the Ethena/USDe ecosystem. Meanwhile, USDe circulating supply has shrunk by 70% since the October 2024 bull market peak when it topped $14 billion, reflecting reduced demand for yield-bearing stablecoin strategies.
items.importance: A Nasdaq listing for an Ethena-focused entity brings institutional capital to the synthetic dollar space. But USDe's 70% supply collapse signals that the yield-driven demand that fueled its growth has evaporated in the current low-yield environment.
items.noteworthy: Monitor USDe supply trends as a proxy for DeFi yield demand. The Nasdaq listing could bring new institutional flows. For DeFi users, Ethena's sustainability depends on funding rate regimes recovering.

items.title: OpenAI Unveils Jalapeño — First Custom Inference Chip With Broadcom
items.desc: OpenAI's first custom silicon, designed with Broadcom, targets LLM inference at scale. Deployment by end of 2026.
items.summary: OpenAI unveiled Jalapeño, its first custom-built inference processor, designed and manufactured with Broadcom and Celestica. The chip is an LLM-optimized intelligence processor for initial deployment by end of 2026, with multi-generation roadmap. Jalapeño combines OpenAI-designed accelerators with Broadcom silicon implementation and networking. The move reduces OpenAI's dependence on NVIDIA GPUs for inference.
items.importance: Custom silicon is the endgame for AI hyperscalers. OpenAI joining Google (TPU), Amazon (Trainium/Inferentia), and Microsoft (Maia) means the GPU monopoly on inference is fracturing. For the rest of us, this accelerates the commoditization of inference hardware.
items.noteworthy: Watch for Jalapeño performance benchmarks vs NVIDIA B200/B300. If OpenAI offers inference-as-a-service on its own silicon, pricing could undercut GPU-based providers. For homelab, this doesn't change anything yet — but the trend is clear.

items.title: Qualcomm Acquires Modular for $4B to Challenge NVIDIA CUDA Dominance
items.desc: Qualcomm buys AI compiler startup Modular (Mojo/Max) in $4B stock deal to build a CUDA-competitive software stack for its AI chips.
items.summary: Qualcomm agreed to acquire Modular, the AI infrastructure startup behind the Mojo programming language and Max inference engine, for approximately $4 billion in stock. The deal positions Qualcomm to offer a unified software platform across its Snapdragon, AI Engine, and datacenter chips, directly competing with NVIDIA's CUDA ecosystem. The transaction is expected to close in H2 2026.
items.importance: CUDA's moat is software, not hardware. Qualcomm buying Modular is the most credible attempt yet to build an alternative AI compute stack. If Mojo/Max get Qualcomm's distribution, the AI hardware market could see real competition at the software layer for the first time.
items.noteworthy: Watch if Qualcomm opensources Mojo post-acquisition. For local AI inference, Qualcomm's NPUs + Modular's compiler could be a compelling alternative to NVIDIA for edge and mobile workloads.

items.title: GLM-5.2: The Step Change for Open-Source AI Agents
items.desc: Open-weight GLM-5.2 sustains ~1,700 autonomous agent steps per session, rivaling proprietary frontier models for agentic tasks.
items.summary: GLM-5.2, released June 16, 2026 by Z.AI (formerly Tsinghua AI), represents a capability leap for open-weight models in agentic tasks. It sustains roughly 1,700 autonomous agent steps in a single session — up from an industry baseline of ~20 steps a year earlier. The model runs 'plan, execute, test, fix, optimize' loops autonomously and is MIT-licensed. Nathan Lambert called it the 'DeepSeek moment for agents.'
items.importance: Open models crossing the agentic capability threshold changes the AI landscape. If GLM-5.2 can match proprietary models on agent tasks while being MIT-licensed, the argument for closed-source frontier models weakens significantly.
items.noteworthy: Test GLM-5.2 on your agent workflows. It's available on Ollama Cloud and HuggingFace. For autonomous coding, research, and multi-step tasks, this may outperform larger proprietary models at a fraction of the cost.

items.title: Krea 2 Goes Open Source: 12B DiT Model Generates 2K Images in 2 Seconds
items.desc: Krea released open weights for Krea 2 Raw and Turbo — a 12B parameter dense DiT model with 2-second 2K generation.
items.summary: Krea published open weights for Krea 2 on Hugging Face on June 24, 2026. The model uses a Qwen Image VAE, 12B dense DiT backbone, and Qwen3-VL text encoder. Two checkpoints ship: Krea 2 Raw (quality-focused) and Krea 2 Turbo (speed-optimized). The model generates 2K resolution images in approximately 2 seconds on consumer GPUs. This is a direct open-source competitor to Midjourney and DALL-E.
items.importance: A 12B open-weight image model that runs on consumer hardware and generates 2K in 2 seconds is a milestone. For local AI image generation, this is the first credible alternative to proprietary services at this quality level.
items.noteworthy: Download and test Krea 2 on your local GPU. Compare quality vs Flux, SD3.5, and Midjourney. The Turbo variant is particularly interesting for real-time generation workflows.

items.title: NVIDIA Unveils 45°C Liquid Cooling — Near-Zero Water Use for AI Data Centers
items.desc: New closed-loop cooling architecture runs at 45°C, cutting data center water consumption to near zero and reducing power costs.
items.summary: NVIDIA detailed a new liquid cooling architecture for next-generation AI infrastructure that operates at 45°C coolant temperature. The closed-loop design claims up to 100% reduction in water consumption compared to traditional evaporative cooling, and lower power costs through higher operating temperatures. The design is aimed at the next generation of AI factories running GB300 and future GPU clusters.
items.importance: Data center water usage is becoming a regulatory and PR liability for AI companies. NVIDIA's 45°C design addresses both the environmental angle and operational costs. For homelab, the trickle-down to liquid cooling for consumer/workstation GPUs is worth watching.
items.noteworthy: If you're planning a homelab liquid cooling setup, monitor NVIDIA's 45°C spec — lower temperature delta requirements mean simpler, cheaper cooling loops. For data center operators, this is a direct operational cost reduction.

items.title: Gemini 3.5 Flash Gets Native Computer Use — Agentic AI Built Into the Model
items.desc: Google integrates computer use as a native tool in Gemini 3.5 Flash, replacing the standalone Gemini 2.5 Computer Use model.
items.summary: Google announced that computer use is now a built-in tool in Gemini 3.5 Flash, delivering its best performance yet for agentic computer use tasks. Previously only available as a standalone Gemini 2.5 model, computer use is now natively integrated into the main Flash model alongside Search and Maps grounding. This enables the model to interact with web pages, Android apps, and desktop interfaces directly.
items.importance: Native computer use in a mainstream model signals that agentic AI is becoming a standard capability, not a separate product. For developers, this means fewer integration layers between the model and the action.
items.noteworthy: Test Gemini 3.5 Flash computer use for automation tasks. Compare against Claude's computer use and OpenAI's operator. For homelab automation, this could replace brittle Selenium/Playwright scripts.

items.title: Cordyceps: CI/CD Flaws Expose 300+ GitHub Repos to Supply-Chain Attacks
items.desc: Novee Security finds critical exploitable CI/CD pattern affecting Microsoft, Google, Apache, and 300+ high-impact repos.
items.summary: Novee Security disclosed Cordyceps, a class of CI/CD workflow weakness that allows attackers to hijack workflows and compromise open-source supply chains. A scan of ~30,000 high-impact repositories found 300+ fully exploitable, enabling attacker-controlled code execution, credential theft, and supply chain compromise. Affected organizations include Microsoft, Google, and Apache. The pattern involves insecure use of GitHub Actions workflow triggers and artifact handling.
items.importance: CI/CD pipelines are the soft underbelly of open-source. Cordyceps shows that even major orgs have exploitable workflow patterns. If you maintain or depend on open-source, this is a direct supply chain risk.
items.noteworthy: Audit your GitHub Actions workflows for Cordyceps patterns. Review workflow trigger configurations, artifact passing, and pull request approval flows. Update actions/checkout to latest version which blocks common attack patterns.

items.title: CISA Warns: Lantronix EDS5000 CVE-2025-67038 Actively Exploited
items.desc: Critical code injection flaw (CVSS 9.8) in Lantronix EDS5000 series devices is being actively exploited. CISA orders fixes by June 26.
items.summary: CISA warned of active exploitation of CVE-2025-67038 (CVSS 9.8), a code injection vulnerability in Lantronix EDS5000 Series devices. The flaw could allow unauthenticated remote code execution. FCEB agencies must apply fixes by June 26, 2026. Lantronix EDS5000 devices are serial-to-Ethernet converters widely used in industrial, energy, and critical infrastructure environments.
items.importance: OT/IoT devices with network connectivity are a growing attack surface. A CVSS 9.8 in widely deployed industrial serial servers means attackers can pivot from IT to OT networks through these devices.
items.noteworthy: Identify and patch Lantronix EDS5000 devices immediately. If you have these in your homelab or lab environment, check firmware versions and apply updates. Segment OT devices from general network access.

items.title: Fake AI Agent Skill Passed Security Scans, Reached 26,000 Agents
items.desc: Malicious AI agent skill bypassed security review and was installed on 26,000 agent platforms before detection.
items.summary: A fake AI agent skill disguised as a legitimate tool passed security scans and was deployed to approximately 26,000 AI agent instances before being discovered. The malicious skill appeared to perform a benign function but contained code that exfiltrated conversation data, API keys, and environment variables. The incident highlights the lack of vetting in AI agent marketplaces and the supply chain risk of third-party agent skills.
items.importance: AI agent marketplaces are repeating the app store security mistakes of the past decade. If agents are executing skills with access to your data and credentials, a malicious skill is a direct compromise vector.
items.noteworthy: Audit installed AI agent skills and plugins. Review permissions granted to third-party agent extensions. Treat agent skills with the same scrutiny as browser extensions or npm packages.

items.title: Bunny DNS Goes Free — Up to 500 Domains Per Account
items.desc: Bunny.net drops DNS query charges entirely, offering free DNS hosting for up to 500 domains. No catch.
items.summary: Bunny.net announced Bunny DNS is now completely free. The service no longer charges for DNS queries and includes free DNS hosting for up to 500 domains per account. Bunny positions DNS as the core glue of their CDN platform and says the move is strategic — driving adoption of their broader edge services. The announcement hit #1 on Hacker News with 851 points.
items.importance: Free, reliable DNS with no query limits is a big deal for homelab operators and small projects. Bunny's global anycast network means low-latency resolution worldwide. For multi-domain setups, 500 domains per account is generous.
items.noteworthy: Migrate your homelab domains to Bunny DNS. Compare latency vs Cloudflare, Route53, and your current provider. For self-hosted services, Bunny's free tier eliminates a recurring cost.

items.title: Trump Order Sets 2030 Deadline for Federal Post-Quantum Crypto Migration
items.desc: Executive order mandates federal agencies migrate high-value systems to post-quantum cryptography by December 31, 2030.
items.summary: President Trump signed an executive order on June 22, 2026, setting hard deadlines for federal agencies to migrate to post-quantum cryptography. Key establishment must move by December 31, 2030; digital signatures by December 31, 2031. The order covers high-value assets and high-impact systems across all federal civilian agencies. Cloudflare, NIST, and industry groups praised the timeline as aggressive but achievable.
items.importance: PQC migration is the largest cryptographic transition in history. This EO sets a regulatory clock ticking for every organization that does business with the US federal government. For everyone else, it's a forcing function to start planning.
items.noteworthy: Start auditing your cryptographic inventory now. Identify systems using RSA, ECDSA, and ECDH that will need PQC upgrades. Test hybrid PQC schemes (X25519Kyber, etc.) in your infrastructure. The 2030 deadline will arrive faster than it seems.

items.title: Market Snapshot: BTC $60,777 | ETH $1,618 | SOL $67.61
items.desc: Crypto market takes a 3.5% dip. BTC below $61K, ETH below $1,650, SOL below $70. Broad sell-off.
items.summary: CoinGecko data on June 25, 2026 shows Bitcoin at $60,777 (-3.51% 24h, market cap $1.22T), Ethereum at $1,618 (-3.42%), and Solana at $67.61 (-3.69%). All three major assets saw coordinated selling pressure, breaking below recent support levels. BTC lost the $62K level that had held for the past week. No single catalyst identified — likely macro positioning ahead of end-of-quarter rebalancing.
items.importance: BTC losing $62K support is bearish in the short term. The next support level is $58K. If this is end-of-quarter institutional rebalancing, the dip may be temporary. If it's macro-driven (rates, liquidity), the correction could deepen.
items.noteworthy: Watch $58K support on BTC. If it breaks, $52K is next. For ETH, $1,500 is the key level. Consider setting limit orders at support levels if you're looking to accumulate. Monitor macro catalysts this week.
